plainMeaning: Here the reciter reviews the fifteen most violent ways of earning a living (karmādāna) and then confesses the eighth vow — refraining from 'pointless harm,' the needless damage we cause through bad thoughts, careless play, handing others weapons, and giving sinful advice.
recitation: Kammato talāra, guptapāla pramukha khara karma ane ājīvikā arthe ku-vyavasāya pariharuṁ. Kammato e vratataṇā pandara aticāra śodhuṁ — iṅgāla-kamme, vaṇa-kamme, sāḍī-kamme, bhāḍī-kamme, phoḍī-kamme, danta-vāṇijje, lakkha-vāṇijje, rasa-vāṇijje, visa-vāṇijje, kesa-vāṇijje, janta-pīlaṇa-kamme, nillaṁchaṇa-kamme, davaggi-dāvaṇayā, sara-daha-talāva-sosaṇayā, asai-posaṇayā. E pannare karmādāna viṣe sthūla-niyama sūkṣmataṇī jayaṇā. Āṭhamuṁ anartha-daṇḍa-viramaṇa-vrata caturvidha — avajjhāṇā-carie, pamāyā-yarie, hiṁsappayāṇe, pāva-kammovaese. Āratta-dhyāna, raudra-dhyāna apadhyānācarita; jīlaṇuṁ-chāṭaṇuṁ-dyūta-hoḍa-vāda-vikathādika pramādācarita; kośa-kodāḷo-pāvaḍo-dhanuṣa-khaḍga-āga āpīe te hiṁsā-pradāna; ghoḍā-kalhoḍā samarāva, khetra-kheḍa, gāḍuṁ-vāha, hāṭa-māṇḍa isyuṁ je bolīe te pāpopadeśa. E anartha-daṇḍa-viramaṇa-vratataṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ — kandappe, kukkuie, moharie, saṁjuttāhigaraṇo, uvabhoga-paribhoga-airege.

wordByWord.gloss: wicked/injurious occupation or enterprise
wordByWord.notes: The umbrella term the verse uses for the harmful trades it is about to enumerate.

wordByWord.gloss: I renounce / I keep away from
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pari- + √hṛ ('to carry away, abandon'); Gujarati 1st-person pariharuṁ
wordByWord.notes: The performative verb of renunciation that governs the whole karmādāna list.

wordByWord.gloss: fifteen
wordByWord.notes: Fixes the canonical count of the karmādāna at fifteen.

wordByWord.gloss: transgression(s) of a vow
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ati- ('beyond') + √car ('to move'); the book's spelling atiyāra is preserved
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; here the fifteen karmādāna are framed as the atichāras under review.

wordByWord.gloss: I scrutinise / cleanse / examine
wordByWord.notes: The recurring purification-formula verb opening each block.

wordByWord.gloss: charcoal/fire-work (1st karmādāna): kilns, smithies, firing of metal, bricks, lime
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit iṅgāla (Skt. aṅgāra, 'charcoal/ember') + kamma (Skt. karma, 'work'); locative -e
wordByWord.notes: Expanded in the verse as foundry, smith, brazier, kiln-firing, metal-smelting — all fire-based trades.

wordByWord.gloss: forest/vegetation-trade (2nd): dealing grain, cotton, flowers, fruit, leaves; felling timber, bamboo
wordByWord.notes: Includes milling and getting bamboo, vines and wood cut and split.

wordByWord.gloss: cart-work (3rd): making and trading carts, wagons, axles and their parts
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śakaṭa ('cart'); the Prakrit list-form sāḍī-kamme is glossed by the verse's expansion as śakaṭa-karma
wordByWord.notes: The Prakrit list-token and the numbered expansion differ in spelling; preserved verbatim.

wordByWord.gloss: hire/transport-work (4th): loading pack-animals — oxen, camels, donkeys, mules — for freight
wordByWord.notes: Profit from over-burdening hired beasts of carriage.

wordByWord.gloss: breaking/digging-work (5th): quarrying stone, mining, digging earth and clods, ploughing
wordByWord.notes: Violence to earth-bodied (pṛthvīkāya) beings through excavation.

wordByWord.gloss: ivory/animal-part trade (6th): tusks, chowries, musk, claws, hair, hides obtained from the source
wordByWord.notes: Condemned because procuring such goods at the 'āgara' (source) directly incites killing.

wordByWord.gloss: lac-trade (7th): lac, indigo, realgar, dhāvaḍī, mahuḍā, washing-soda, soap, etc.
wordByWord.notes: Products whose making destroys countless minute organisms.

wordByWord.gloss: liquids-trade (8th): selling juices, honey, butter, fats and the like
wordByWord.notes: Honey and butter especially, as their extraction/use involves harm to beings.

wordByWord.gloss: poison/weapon trade (9th): poison, ploughs, weapons, iron tools, orpiment, combs — life-destroying goods
wordByWord.notes: Any 'jīva-ghātaka vastu' (creature-killing article) sold or got sold.

wordByWord.gloss: hair/living-being trade (10th): trafficking in two-footed (humans) and four-footed (animals)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kesa (Skt. keśa, 'hair'); here construed as trade in hair-bearing living beings
wordByWord.notes: The verse glosses it as sale of dvipada (bipeds) and catuṣpada (quadrupeds).

wordByWord.gloss: machine-pressing work (11th): water-wheels, oil/cane presses, querns, mortars, mills run
wordByWord.notes: Crushing implements that pulverise countless beings.

wordByWord.gloss: mutilation work (12th): branding, castrating, ear-cutting, nose-piercing of bipeds and quadrupeds
wordByWord.notes: Deliberate disfiguring of animals (and humans) for use or profit.

wordByWord.gloss: forest-fire setting (13th): setting or having grass-and-crop fields burnt for greater yield
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit davaggi (Skt. davāgni, 'wildfire') + dāpana ('causing to give/set')
wordByWord.notes: Mass destruction of fire-, air- and mobile beings by arson of vegetation.

wordByWord.gloss: drying of waters (14th): draining lakes, tanks, deep pools, channels
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saras ('lake') + hrada ('pool') + talāva ('tank') + śoṣaṇa ('drying up')
wordByWord.notes: Annihilates water-bodied (apkāya) and aquatic beings.

wordByWord.gloss: rearing for ill purpose (15th): keeping dogs, cats, parrots, peacocks, fowl, and servants for immoral/cruel use
wordByWord.notes: Maintaining creatures or people for vice, exploitation or violence; closes with 'and other sinful trades'.

wordByWord.gloss: these fifteen karmādāna (highly injurious occupations)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pannara ('fifteen') + Skt. karma-ādāna ('that which draws in karma')
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term karmādāna; summary tag closing the trade-list.

wordByWord.gloss: a gross (firm) restraint, with sparing care as to the subtle
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sthūla ('gross') + niyama ('rule'); sūkṣma ('subtle'); jayaṇā (Skt. yatanā, 'careful restraint')
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term jayaṇā; the householder vows the gross abstention while exercising care where total avoidance is impossible.

wordByWord.gloss: eighth
wordByWord.notes: Marks the eighth of the twelve lay vows.

wordByWord.gloss: the vow of refraining from purposeless harm
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. an-artha ('purposeless/without benefit') + daṇḍa ('harm, punishment, infliction') + viramaṇa ('desisting') + vrata
wordByWord.notes: The third guṇavrata; daṇḍa here = self-inflicted karmic damage incurred for no real need.

wordByWord.gloss: fourfold
wordByWord.notes: Announces the four species of purposeless harm that follow.

wordByWord.gloss: conduct of evil meditation (apadhyānācarita) — dwelling in malevolent thought
wordByWord.notes: Expanded as ārtadhyāna and raudradhyāna and brooding on quarrel, curses, harsh speech.

wordByWord.gloss: conduct of negligence (pramādācarita) — heedless, idle, dissipating activity
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pamāya (Skt. pramāda, 'negligence, intoxication of carelessness') + ācarita
wordByWord.notes: Verse lists gambling, idle sports, frivolous shows, animal-fights, oversleeping, needless plucking, temple defilement.

wordByWord.gloss: supplying instruments of violence (hiṁsā-pradāna) — lending weapons or tools of harm
wordByWord.notes: Verse: handing out spade, hoe, shovel, bow, sword, fire to others.

wordByWord.gloss: sinful advice (pāpa-karma-upadeśa) — counselling others toward harmful action
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pāva (Skt. pāpa, 'sin') + kamma (karma) + uvaesa (Skt. upadeśa, 'instruction')
wordByWord.notes: Verse: urging others to ready horses, plough fields, drive carts, set up shops.

wordByWord.gloss: anguished/sorrowful meditation (the lowest inauspicious meditation)
wordByWord.notes: First of the two durdhyānas illustrating apadhyānācarita.

wordByWord.gloss: cruel/violent meditation
wordByWord.notes: Second inauspicious meditation; brooding on injury, theft, possessiveness.

wordByWord.gloss: gambling, betting/wagers, contentious disputes, idle/harmful gossip
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. dyūta ('dice/gambling'); hoḍa ('wager'); vāda ('disputation'); vi-kathā ('worthless talk')
wordByWord.notes: Representative instances of pramādācarita.

wordByWord.gloss: the ten disrespects (defilements of temple/upāśraya)
wordByWord.notes: E.g. chewing betel (tāmbūla) in the temple; cited as a form of pramādācarita.

wordByWord.token: કોશ, કોદાલો, પાવડો, ધનુષ, ખડ્ગ, આગ
wordByWord.translit: kośa, kodāḷo, pāvaḍo, dhanuṣa, khaḍga, āga
wordByWord.gloss: spade, mattock/hoe, shovel, bow, sword, fire
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. khanitra/kuddāla, dhanuṣ, khaḍga, agni — Gujar+icised forms
wordByWord.notes: The harmful implements whose lending constitutes hiṁsā-pradāna.

wordByWord.gloss: the five transgressions (of this vow)
wordByWord.notes: Introduces the canonical five atichāras of anartha-daṇḍa.

wordByWord.gloss: kandarpa (1st atichāra): lustful, suggestive speech and frivolity
wordByWord.notes: Verse: 'savikāra vacana' — speaking with sensual gestures/innuendo.

wordByWord.gloss: kaukucya (2nd): clownish buffoonery — facial/eye antics to make people laugh
wordByWord.notes: Verse glosses as bhāṇḍa-ceṣṭā (mimicry/buffoon's gestures).

wordByWord.gloss: maukharya (3rd): garrulous, impudent, untruthful chatter
wordByWord.notes: Verse: prattling cutting, vulgar or false words from sheer volubility.

wordByWord.gloss: saṁyuktādhikaraṇa (4th): keeping implements of harm assembled and ready
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṁyukta ('joined together') + adhikaraṇa ('instrument/apparatus of action')
wordByWord.notes: Verse: leaving mortar-pestle, mill, bow-arrow, yoke-goad lying joined and usable; also over-attachment in bathing/eating/dressing.

wordByWord.gloss: upabhoga-paribhoga-atireka (5th): excess in things of single-use and repeated enjoyment
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. upabhoga ('one-time consumable, e.g. food') + paribhoga ('reusable, e.g. clothes') + atireka ('excess')
wordByWord.notes: Accumulating/using more enjoyables than need warrants — needless harm by surplus.

wordByWord.gloss: ruthless/groundless speech — i.e. false speech
wordByWord.notes: Closing example of speech-faults under this vow; reading uncertain (see notes).

literalTranslation: I renounce wicked livelihoods such as those of the talāra and guptapāla and the like, undertaken for hard gain or mere subsistence. By way of occupation I now scrutinise the fifteen transgressions of this vow: charcoal-work, forest-work, cart-work, transport-work, breaking-work, ivory-trade, lac-trade, juice-trade, poison-trade, living-being-trade, machine-pressing-work, mutilation-work, wildfire-setting, the drying of lakes-pools-tanks, and rearing for immoral purpose. (1) Charcoal-work — for gain I made foundry, smith's, goldsmith's, brazier's, tinker's, parcher's, brick- and lime-firing, metal-smelting and such fire-work; … (15) immoral rearing — I kept dogs, cats, parrots, peacocks, fowl, ill-conducted servants and bond-folk, and did many other sinful trades and enterprises. As to these fifteen karmādāna I hold a firm gross restraint with sparing care for the subtle. Eighth: the vow of refraining from purposeless harm, fourfold — conduct of ill-meditation, conduct of negligence, supplying instruments of violence, and sinful advice. (1) Anguished and cruel meditation held even for a moment, and brooding on enmity, quarrel, sulking, curses, abuse and harsh words — that is apadhyānācarita. (2) Swinging, splashing, dicing, wagering, disputing, idle gossip, and (barring acts of meritorious propagation) theatricals, songs, revels, sports, costumes, and setting wrestlers, buffaloes, dogs and cocks to fight, sleeping the whole night out of indolence, needlessly plucking flower-fruit-leaf, the ten disrespects such as betel-chewing in temple or hall — that is pramādācarita. (3) Out of social obligation lending spade, hoe, shovel, bow, sword, fire — that is hiṁsā-pradāna. (4) Saying 'ready the horses and mares, plough the field, drive the cart, set up the shop' — that is pāpopadeśa. Refraining from this fourfold purposeless harm, I now scrutinise the five transgressions of the eighth vow: kandarpa, kaukucya, maukharya, saṁyuktādhikaraṇa, and excess of enjoyables and reusables — speaking lustfully (kandarpa); buffoonish mimicry and facial-ocular antics that make people laugh (kaukucya); garrulous, vulgar or false prattle (maukharya); leaving harmful implements joined and ready, and excessive indulgence in bath, food and dress, or dressing the hair, or plastering with stale matter at night (saṁyuktādhikaraṇa); and ruthless or untrue speech — and whatever else I did against this eighth vow.
idiomaticTranslation: I turn away from every cruel and bloody livelihood — the trades of the jailer, the warden, and their kind — pursued for hard gain or mere bread. So, vow by vow, let me lay bare the fifteen most violent occupations: firing kilns, stripping forests, building carts, driving freight-beasts, quarrying earth; dealing in ivory and animal parts, in lac, in oils and honey, in poison and weapons, in living creatures; running crushing-mills, branding and castrating beasts, torching fields, draining lakes, and keeping creatures and servants for vice. Where I cannot wholly abstain, I hold at least the gross restraint with sparing care. Now the eighth vow — refusing harm that serves no purpose — which is fourfold: a mind that broods in anguish or cruelty; a life squandered in gambling, idle shows, animal-baiting and sloth; hands that lend out blade, bow and fire; and a tongue that eggs others on to sin. Confessing these, I examine the vow's five subtler slips: lewd talk, clownish antics that play for laughs, loose and lying chatter, leaving instruments of harm lying ready, and heaping up far more comforts than I need.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This single long verse executes the 'action-aspect' (karmataḥ) of the bhogopabhoga discipline and then opens the eighth vow, so it reads as two linked confessions. First come the fifteen karmādāna — not a random catalogue but a systematic survey of livelihoods that necessarily mass-destroy life across all six classes of beings: fire-work and wildfire strike tejaskāya; quarrying and digging strike pṛthvīkāya; draining waters strikes apkāya; forest- and machine-work strikes vanaspatikāya; and the animal trades, mutilation and 'immoral rearing' strike the mobile (trasa) beings directly. The verse's vivid Gujarati expansions (foundries, oil-presses, branding, the keeping of fowl and bond-servants) are deliberately concrete so a merchant recognises his own ledger in them. Crucially it ends each restraint with sthūla-niyama … jayaṇā: the householder pledges the gross abstention while honestly admitting the subtle harm he cannot escape — the hallmark of lay, as against ascetic, vows. The verse then pivots to anartha-daṇḍa, and the genius of the eighth vow is its target: not harm done for survival (that the earlier vows already limit) but daṇḍa — self-punishment — incurred for no benefit at all. Its fourfold analysis is a complete moral anatomy: thought (apadhyāna), undisciplined activity (pramāda), enabling others' violence (hiṁsā-pradāna), and speech that incites (pāpopadeśa) — mind, body, others'-hands, and tongue. The five atichāras then catch the residue that slips past even a sincere vow: lewd wit (kandarpa), buffoonery (kaukucya), loose talk (maukharya), tools left lying ready (saṁyuktādhikaraṇa), and surplus consumption (upabhoga-paribhoga-atireka). Note how the same crushing implements condemned among the karmādāna reappear here as saṁyuktādhikaraṇa — the verse quietly teaches that even merely keeping such things 'assembled and ready' is a fault short of using them.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain doctrine that karmic bondage is governed less by the outward deed than by intention and by the needlessness of the harm. The aṇuvratas restrain harm tied to legitimate need; the eighth guṇavrata closes the larger, easier leak — the gratuitous harm of an idle, indulgent or loose-tongued life. Hence anartha ('purpose-less') is the operative word: the same act may be daṇḍa-worthy or excusable depending on whether any real artha justifies it. The fourfold scheme (apadhyāna, pramāda, hiṁsā-pradāna, pāpopadeśa) and the five atichāras are the standard śrāvaka formulation also systematised by Umāsvāti and elaborated by Hemacandra in the Yogaśāstra; the karmādāna list belongs to the bhogopabhoga-paribhoga vow of the same canonical scheme. The pairing of sthūla restraint with jayaṇā encodes the realistic gradualism of the lay path: perfection of ahiṁsā is the monk's, scrupulous minimisation the householder's.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Read today, the karmādāna become a conscience-test for one's profession and investments — does my livelihood depend on mass violence, pollution or trafficking? — while jayaṇā licenses honest, incremental reduction rather than paralysing all-or-nothing demands. The eighth vow is strikingly modern: it indicts doom-scrolling and violent entertainment (pramādācarita), online cruelty and inciting comments (pāpopadeśa and maukharya), careless lending of harmful means, and over-consumption (upabhoga-paribhoga-atireka). A reciter applies the verse by auditing not just deliberate wrongs but the wasteful, purposeless harm woven through ordinary leisure, speech and shopping.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.21 (anarthadaṇḍa among the vrata) and 7.26–7.27 (atichāras of bhogopabhoga and anartha-daṇḍa: kandarpa, kautkucya, maukharya, saṁyuktādhikaraṇa, upabhoga-paribhogātireka)

notes: This is an unusually long prose verse; word-by-word coverage groups the verse's vivid illustrative item-lists (trade-tools, animals, foodstuffs) under their governing technical token rather than glossing each obscure regional craft-term separately, since the doctrinal load sits on the Prakrit/Sanskrit category names. The Prakrit list-forms and their numbered Gujarati expansions sometimes differ in spelling (e.g. sāḍī-kamme → śakaṭa-karma; bhāḍī-kamme → bhāṭa-karma; phoḍī- → phoṭi-; janta-pīlaṇa → yantra-pīlaṇa), preserved verbatim per the source-book's fidelity rule. 'kesa-vāṇijje' is glossed by the verse itself as trade in bipeds/quadrupeds (living beings), though the literal sense is 'hair-trade'; both noted. The closing token 'nikkaṁśa-vacana' is a rare/uncertain reading (possibly niḥśaṅka- 'unrestrained' or a variant) and the verse equates it with asatya-vacana; flagged as low-confidence. The recitation field renders the principal recited skeleton (Prakrit category-lists, the fourfold and the five-atichāra lines) in IAST; the long Gujarati explanatory expansions are rendered in the word-by-word and translations rather than re-transliterated in full. The Tattvārtha-sūtra atichāra numbering (7.26–27) follows the standard Śvetāmbara recension; verse references to Yogaśāstra 3.73/113 are as cited in the Cologne lexicon entry consulted this session.
